nativeRange.habitat: Found in shallow, well-protected coastal bays, typically hiding under jetties and in other darkened areas. Also occurs in inshore rocky zones and among branching coral.

diet.notes: Has been observed readily accepting meaty frozen and prepared foods including enriched brine shrimp, enriched mysis shrimp, marine pellets, and flake foods.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Rickard Zerpe, 3686x2765. Sharp, well-lit in-situ shot of the whole fish showing the diagnostic deep dark/blackish coloration and large dark eye; only clean live-specimen photo available.

summary: The Black Cardinalfish is a small, slender reef fish with deep black coloration and large dark eyes. Generally considered peaceful and well-suited to reef aquariums, it tends to shelter among rockwork, sea urchins, or seagrass and is broadly regarded as a hardy, beginner-friendly species.
careNotes: Black Cardinalfish tend to hide among rockwork, sea urchins, long-spined urchins, or seagrass beds in the aquarium, so ample sheltered areas are generally advised. Groups have been reported to establish a loose hierarchy without notable aggression toward one another. The species is a paternal mouthbrooder with distinct pairing behavior during courtship and spawning, which has occasionally been replicated in captivity.
breedingNotes: Has been documented as a paternal mouthbrooder exhibiting distinct pairing during courtship and spawning. Captive breeding is rarely reported in detail but is considered possible in a species-appropriate aquarium.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size, native range, temperature, taxonomy (order Kurtiformes), and habitat. Temperature disagreement noted: FishBase reports preferred wild habitat temperature of 27.1–29.3°C vs LiveAquaria 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C). Averaged range used; dataStatus set to needs_review due to this spread.

fish.breedingNotes: The Black Cardinalfish is a paternal mouthbrooder, with distinct pairing behavior during courtship and spawning. Captive breeding is rarely reported in home aquaria.
